title: History of the Congo (Democratic Republic)

stack.label: Congo Free State (Leopold II — personal colony)
Democratic Republic of the Congo
stack.note: 1964–1971; Mobutu Sese Seko rises to power

stack.label: Democratic Republic of the Congo
stack.note: 1997–; Laurent-Désiré Kabila → Joseph Kabila → Félix Tshisekedi

footnotes: Kingdom of Kongo (c. 1390–1914): One of the largest and most powerful pre-colonial African states, centred at Mbanza Kongo (São Salvador); converted to Christianity after Portuguese contact (1483); maintained diplomatic relations with European powers; gradually fragmented under Portuguese, then Belgian, pressure.
Luba Kingdom (c. 1585–1889): A centralised state in the Katanga-Kasai region known for its sacred kingship (Mulopwe) and sophisticated political culture; conquered by Belgian forces in 1889.

Lunda Kingdom (c. 1665–1887): An expansive empire that spread Lunda political culture across much of central Africa; founded by Mwata Yamvo; its sub-kingdoms extended into present-day Angola and Zambia.

Kuba Kingdom (c. 1625–1900): A confederation of Bantu-speaking peoples in the Kasai region famous for its intricate raffia textiles and art; resisted external domination longer than most central African kingdoms.

Congo Free State (1885–1908): Leopold II's personal private colony, established at the Berlin Conference (1884–85); the site of one of history's worst colonial atrocities — forced rubber quotas enforced by mutilation and mass killing; international outcry led to Belgian state annexation in 1908.

Belgian Congo (1908–1960): After Belgium assumed control, some of the worst abuses were curtailed but racial segregation and economic exploitation continued; uranium from the Congo was used in the Manhattan Project; independence granted 30 June 1960.
Katanga Secession (1960–1963): Mineral-rich Katanga province declared independence under Moïse Tshombe days after independence; UN intervened; ended with military reintegration in 1963.

Zaire / Mobutu era (1971–1997): Mobutu Sese Seko renamed the country Zaire and instituted Authenticité (African authenticity) nationalism; ruled as a kleptocrat backed by the West during the Cold War; regime collapsed during the First Congo War.

Congo Wars (1996–2003): First Congo War (1996–97) ended Mobutu's rule; Second Congo War (1998–2003), called 'Africa's World War', involved nine nations and resulted in an estimated 5.4 million deaths, the deadliest conflict since World War II.

Eastern Congo instability (2003–present): Despite formal peace, eastern Congo (Kivu provinces) has remained a conflict zone with dozens of armed groups; M23 rebellion (2012–13, resurgent 2022–) backed by Rwanda remains an ongoing crisis.
